metrics.narrative: Adeel Ahmed articulated the business problem on May 25, 2025: 'We have a significant amount of manual three-way matching happening in AP... receipting is done manually in the warehouse... we want to automate that process to free up resources.' He quantified scale ('about 50,000 invoices a year') but provided no quantified financial impact (cost per invoice, FTE hours, error rate, cycle time, or savings target). On Jun 17, 2026 he reframed the problem as change-management-sensitive: 'it's a bit sensitive... everyone's gonna start getting spooked that jobs are going... the idea is not to get rid of anyone's jobs, we wanna rebrand or redirect resources.' No ROI target, payback period, or cost-per-transaction baseline has been shared. The buyer has not completed the internal AP-process audit (a buyer-owned deliverable) to document current-state metrics (workflow, headcount, cycle time, error rate) that would ground ROI modeling. Without quantified metrics, the business case remains qualitative and vulnerable to a 'compelling, cost-effective' test (Adeel's words, May 25) that may favor the bundled Oracle P2P add-on.

However, Adeel has not introduced Zycus to the Economic Buyer (CFO), AP team lead, warehouse team lead, or IT/ERP migration lead; he has not pushed back on competitors or driven urgency; and he has explicitly stated 'there's no hard objective' (May 25) and twice deferred the evaluation (Nov 2025, Jan 2026). Champion strength is weak because Adeel is the sole contact (single-threaded), has not mobilized internal advocates, and has not created urgency or a committed timeline. He is developing in that he is invested in the evaluation, but he is not yet an active advocate driving the deal forward.

competition.narrative: The primary competitor is the Oracle Fusion P2P add-on, explicitly named by Adeel Ahmed as the alternative on May 25, 2025 ('Oracle P2P add-on versus Zycus') and reaffirmed on Jun 17, 2026 ('I need to brief the CFO on the Zycus vs Oracle P2P add-on evaluation'). Oracle Fusion is Alfred Health's target ERP platform (migrating from the relevant stakeholder by 2029), so the Oracle P2P add-on is the in-house, bundled option and the default choice if Zycus does not present a compelling, cost-effective business case. Adeel stated on May 25: 'the complication we have investing now while we're gonna be changing systems... cost is one component.' This frames the Oracle add-on as lower-risk (single vendor, guaranteed ERP integration, likely bundled pricing) versus Zycus (separate vendor, integration dependency, standalone pricing). The Salesforce Competitors__c field lists 'Oracle; Unknown/Won't Share', and the Closed_Lost_Reason_Code__c field is not populated (deal is open). How we win: position Zycus Merlin (agentic AI, intelligent three-way matching, exception handling automation) as a differentiated, best-of-breed P2P solution that delivers superior ROI and automation depth versus the Oracle add-on's basic functionality, provide quantified Victorian government and health-sector case studies (Department of Transport, Department of Justice, and address the Monash Health reference concern), and build a compelling business case (ROI, change-management plan, integration roadmap) that Adeel can present to the CFO with Zycus present to counter the Oracle default.

identify_pain.narrative: The specific operational pain is manual, labor-intensive three-way matching and receipting across AP and warehouse teams at Alfred Health Victoria, a large public-sector health system processing approximately 50,000 invoices per year. Adeel Ahmed (Head of Sourcing, pain owner) stated on May 25, 2025: 'We have a significant amount of manual three-way matching happening in AP... receipting is done manually in the warehouse... we want to automate that process to free up resources.' On Jun 17, 2026 he clarified the cultural sensitivity: 'it's a bit sensitive... everyone's gonna start getting spooked that jobs are going... the idea is not to get rid of anyone's jobs, we wanna rebrand or redirect resources.' The pain is confirmed (stated directly) but not quantified (no FTE hours, error rate, cost per invoice, or cycle time shared). The pain is intensified by the upcoming Oracle Fusion ERP migration (2029), which creates a systems-integration complication ('the complication we have investing now while we're gonna be changing systems', May 25) but also defers urgency ('P2P evaluation currently not the top priority', Jan 2026). The pain owner is Adeel Ahmed (Sourcing), with secondary pain felt by the AP team lead and warehouse team lead (neither yet engaged directly).

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is a two-phase internal evaluation led by Adeel Ahmed (Head of Sourcing) with CFO approval required for the commercial decision. Phase 1 (current): Adeel is conducting a 'sensitive investigation' (his words, Jun 17) of current AP and warehouse processes, benchmarking Zycus vs Oracle P2P add-on through demos and business-case development, and gathering input from the AP team lead, warehouse team lead, and IT/ERP migration lead (all referenced on May 25 as stakeholders who 'need to be brought along'). Phase 2 (not yet reached): Adeel will brief the Group CFO on the Zycus vs Oracle evaluation, present a business case (ROI, cost, integration, change-management plan), and secure CFO sign-off for the commercial decision. No procurement or legal approval chain has been discussed. The call sequence shows Adeel as the sole buyer participant across all calls (intro Sept 15, 2025; discovery May 25, 2025; demo Jun 3, 2026; follow-up Jun 17, 2026); no other stakeholders (CFO, AP lead, warehouse lead, IT lead) have joined any call, indicating a single-threaded, early-stage evaluation. Adeel has the authority to qualify and select vendors but not to approve budget.

decision_criteria.narrative: Adeel Ahmed laid out the evaluation criteria explicitly on May 25, 2025 and refined them on Jun 17, 2026. The confirmed criteria are: (1) Automation of manual three-way matching and receipting ('automate the process to free up AP and warehouse resources', May 25); (2) Integration with current ERP (the relevant stakeholder, migrating to Oracle Fusion by 2029) and seamless migration path ('the complication we have investing now while we're gonna be changing systems', May 25); (3) Cost-effectiveness and compelling ROI ('cost is one component... we need a compelling, cost-effective business case', May 25); (4) Change-management fit and staff perception ('it's a bit sensitive... everyone's gonna start getting spooked that jobs are going... the idea is not to rebrand or redirect resources, not eliminate jobs', Jun 17); (5) Exception handling and approval workflow ('when a mismatch happens, who approves it, how does it escalate', Jun 3 demo discussion per Next Step); (6) Supplier onboarding and catalogue management ('how do we onboard suppliers, manage catalogues', May 25). Adeel also referenced the Monash Health peer case as a credibility test ('I heard they had some issues... they're not 100% happy with Zycus', May 25), making peer references an implicit criterion. No formal RFP or BRD has been issued; these are verbally stated evaluation factors.

items.detail: No pricing or commercial discussion has occurred. Adeel stated 'cost is one component' and 'compelling, cost-effective business case' is required (May 25), but no budget range, ROI target, or price sensitivity has been shared. Risk: if Zycus pricing is materially higher than the Oracle P2P add-on (which may be bundled at low/no incremental cost with Oracle Fusion), Adeel may default to Oracle as the lower-cost, lower-risk choice.

Monash Health (Victorian health peer) was mentioned as a Zycus reference, but Adeel noted 'I heard they had some issues... they're not 100% happy with Zycus' (May 25), creating a reference concern. This is a real vulnerability: Monash is a peer organization and the negative perception undermines credibility. Zycus offered Department of Transport and Department of Justice as alternative Victorian government references (May 25) but has not yet provided quantified case studies or facilitated reference calls.

The CFO (Economic Buyer, Group CFO, budget authority) is not yet engaged: no call attendance, no OpportunityContactRole, no commercial or ROI briefing. Adeel has the CFO's blessing to investigate ('I'll have the CFO's blessings to investigate this', Jun 17) but the CFO has not yet seen a demo, proposal, or business case. Risk: if Adeel briefs the CFO without Zycus present, the CFO may default to the Oracle P2P add-on as the safer, bundled, in-house choice.

No committed timeline or compelling event exists. Adeel stated 'there's no hard objective' (May 25) and 'P2P evaluation currently not the top priority... will revisit in March-April' (Jan 2026 Next Step). The close date (2026-12-31, 187 days out) is a placeholder, not a buyer-committed milestone. Risk: the deal may defer indefinitely if the business case is not compelling or if other priorities (Oracle Fusion ERP migration, budget constraints, operational fires) take precedence.

Change-management sensitivity: Adeel is concerned about staff perception ('it's a bit sensitive... everyone's gonna start getting spooked that jobs are going', Jun 17) and explicitly stated 'the idea is not to get rid of anyone's jobs... we wanna rebrand or redirect resources' (May 25). Risk: if the AP team or warehouse team perceives automation as a job threat, they may resist adoption or slow the evaluation. This is a cultural vulnerability in a large, unionized public-sector health system.

Oracle Fusion ERP migration complicates the decision: Alfred Health is migrating from the relevant stakeholder to Oracle Fusion by 2029, and Adeel explicitly framed the decision as 'Oracle P2P add-on versus Zycus' (May 25 and Jun 17). Risk: the Oracle P2P add-on may be perceived as lower-risk (single vendor, bundled with ERP, integration guarantee) vs Zycus (separate vendor, integration dependency). Adeel stated 'the complication we have investing now while we're gonna be changing systems' (May 25).

Single-threaded: Adeel Ahmed is the sole engaged stakeholder (no CFO, AP lead, warehouse lead, or IT/ERP migration lead engaged). Risk: if Adeel leaves, is reassigned, or deprioritizes, the deal stalls. No multi-threading or internal mobilization.
